prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that bats can sense whether an object is moving?

choices.text: the statement that subtle shifts in the returning sound reveal motion toward or away
choices.rationale: Correct. Shifts in the returning sound directly indicate motion.

choices.text: the statement that bats call at pitches too high for humans to hear
choices.rationale: The pitch of the call does not address motion sensing.

choices.text: the statement that the bat judges distance from the delay of the echo
choices.rationale: Echo delay reveals distance, not motion direction.

choices.text: the statement that the world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound
choices.rationale: The 'built of sound' image is general, not motion-specific.

explanation: Motion sensing is supported by the 'subtle shifts' detail.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that the dance conveys distance?

choices.text: the statement that other bees crowd around and follow the dancer closely
choices.rationale: Followers show interest, not the distance code.

choices.text: the statement that the dance passes its information without any sound
choices.rationale: Absence of sound concerns the medium, not distance.

choices.text: the statement that the length of the bee's straight run signals distance
choices.rationale: Correct. Run length signaling distance directly supports the distance claim.

choices.text: the statement that the bee dances on a vertical comb in total darkness
choices.rationale: The setting does not address distance.

explanation: Distance is supported by the 'length of the run' detail.

stimulus.title: Dances in the Dark
stimulus.text: A foraging honeybee that finds a rich patch of flowers cannot simply tell the hive where to go. Instead, it dances. On the vertical comb, in darkness, the bee runs a straight line while waggling its body, then loops back to repeat the move. The angle of that straight run, measured against the pull of gravity, encodes the direction of the flowers relative to the sun. The length of the run signals distance. Watching bees crowd around and follow, researchers realized they were seeing a kind of symbolic language, one that passes precise information without sight, sound, or a single spoken word.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that fog harvesting can meaningfully add to a water supply?

choices.text: the statement that a single large net can collect many liters on a good day
choices.rationale: Correct. The volume collected shows a meaningful contribution.

choices.text: the statement that moist air rolls in toward the hillsides from the sea
choices.rationale: Incoming moist air explains the source, not the amount collected.

choices.text: the statement that the mesh nets require regular upkeep to keep working
choices.rationale: Upkeep is a limitation, not a benefit.

choices.text: the statement that the method works only where fog happens to be frequent
choices.rationale: Frequency is a condition, not the yield.

explanation: The 'many liters' figure supports the usefulness claim.

stimulus.title: Catching Water From Air
stimulus.text: In a few dry coastal regions, drinking water can be harvested from fog. Tall mesh nets are strung across hillsides where moist air rolls in from the sea. As the fog drifts through, droplets cling to the mesh, merge, and trickle down into troughs and storage tanks. A single large net can collect many liters on a good day, enough to supplement a village's supply. The method is not a cure-all: it works only where fog is frequent and winds are steady, and the nets need upkeep. Yet in the right place, it turns a passing cloud into a dependable resource, using little more than gravity and patience.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that a kelp forest can collapse quickly?

choices.text: the statement that giant kelp can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface
choices.rationale: The kelp's height describes its size, not a collapse.

choices.text: the statement that kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters
choices.rationale: Sheltering animals describes richness, not collapse.

choices.text: the statement that without otters, urchins multiply and graze the kelp to bare rock
choices.rationale: Correct. Urchins grazing the kelp to bare rock directly shows a collapse.

choices.text: the statement that the kelp can grow half a meter in a single day
choices.rationale: Fast growth describes vigor, not collapse.

explanation: Collapse is supported by the urchins-graze-to-bare-rock detail.

stimulus.title: Forests Beneath the Waves
stimulus.text: Off some cool, rocky coasts grow forests that no hiker will ever walk through. Giant kelp, anchored to the seafloor, can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface, swaying in the current like trees in wind. These kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters, much as a woodland shelters birds and deer. The kelp grows astonishingly fast, sometimes half a meter in a single day, drinking sunlight near the surface. But the forest is fragile. Where otters vanish, sea urchins multiply and graze the kelp down to bare rock, leaving a barren the divers call an urchin desert. A single missing animal can unmake a whole forest.

prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that coral bands reveal past water temperature?

choices.text: the statement that reading the bands is like reading an unfamiliar language
choices.rationale: The reading-difficulty image does not establish temperature.

choices.text: the statement that the record can stretch back several centuries
choices.rationale: The record's length does not address temperature.

choices.text: the statement that the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature
choices.rationale: Correct. The trapped chemistry directly links the bands to past temperature.

choices.text: the statement that a coral skeleton grows in fine bands like tree rings
choices.rationale: The tree-ring comparison concerns growth, not temperature.

explanation: Temperature is supported specifically by the trapped-chemistry detail.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that some seeds rely on animals eating them?

choices.text: the statement that some seeds grow papery wings and spin on the wind
choices.rationale: Winged seeds rely on wind, not on being eaten.

choices.text: the statement that some seeds wear hooks that snag passing fur
choices.rationale: Hooked seeds rely on fur, not on being eaten.

choices.text: the statement that some seeds hide in sweet fruit so an animal eats and carries them off
choices.rationale: Correct. Hiding in fruit to be eaten and carried directly supports the claim.

choices.text: the statement that a plant cannot walk to find open ground
choices.rationale: The opening notes a plant cannot walk, unrelated to being eaten.

explanation: Reliance on being eaten is supported by the sweet-fruit detail.

stimulus.title: Travelers Without Legs
stimulus.text: A plant cannot walk, yet its seeds must somehow leave the shadow of the parent and find open ground. Evolution has answered this need with a surprising range of tricks. Some seeds grow papery wings and spin away on the wind like tiny helicopters. Others wear hooks that snag the fur of passing animals, hitching a ride for miles. Still others hide inside sweet fruit, counting on an animal to eat them and carry them off, depositing them far away. Each method is a kind of bargain struck with the world around the plant. The seed that travels farthest is often the one with the best chance to grow where the parent's shade cannot reach.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that public voting could intimidate voters?

choices.text: the statement that it is easy to assume voting has always been private
choices.rationale: The opening assumption does not show intimidation.

choices.text: the statement that a worker might think twice before voting against a watching boss
choices.rationale: Correct. The hesitant worker example directly shows intimidation under public voting.

choices.text: the statement that the change shifted power toward the ordinary voter
choices.rationale: The power shift is the effect of reform, not the intimidation itself.

choices.text: the statement that reformers pushed for a printed ballot dropped in a box
choices.rationale: The reformers' push is the response, not evidence of intimidation.

explanation: Intimidation is supported by the watching-boss example.

stimulus.title: The Secret Ballot
stimulus.text: It is easy to assume voting has always been private, but for a long time it was not. In many places people voted aloud or by a show of hands, in full view of employers, landlords, and neighbors. That openness invited pressure: a worker might think twice before voting against a boss who could see the choice. Reformers pushed for a printed ballot, marked alone and dropped in a box, so that no one need fear retaliation for an honest vote. The change seemed minor, a mere matter of paper, yet it shifted real power toward the ordinary voter, who could now decide without an audience.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that locks rely on simple means?

choices.text: the statement that shallow rivers became highways of commerce
choices.rationale: Rivers becoming highways is an effect, not evidence of simplicity.

choices.text: the statement that distant towns became trading partners
choices.rationale: Trading partnerships are an effect, not evidence of simplicity.

choices.text: the statement that a boat enters a chamber with gates at each end
choices.rationale: The chamber description explains how a lock works, not its simplicity.

choices.text: the statement that the lock relies on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience
choices.rationale: Correct. 'Nothing more exotic than gravity and patience' directly shows simple means.

explanation: Simplicity is supported by the 'gravity and patience' detail.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.
